summary: A 47-year-old man with epigastric pain who underwent upper endoscopy showing duodenal ulcer and a positive H. pylori biopsy asks his primary nurse practitioner about treatment options. He has no penicillin allergy and has not taken antibiotics in the last several years. The held-out answer must reflect ACG 2024 H. pylori treatment guideline — bismuth quadruple therapy as preferred first-line in regions of high macrolide resistance (now including most of the United States), the 14-day duration, the test-of-cure timing (>=4 weeks after completion of therapy and >=2 weeks off PPI), and the importance of ulcer healing follow-up.

messages.content: I had an endoscopy for stomach pain last week. They found a duodenal ulcer and the biopsy was positive for H. pylori. The GI office mentioned a 14-day antibiotic regimen. What's the actual plan, and how do we know it worked?

do_NOT: describe clarithromycin triple therapy as universally first-line in 2026 (it isn't, in most US regions, due to clarithromycin resistance)
describe a 7-day course as adequate for first-line therapy in current guidance
skip the test-of-cure timing requirements (>=4 weeks post-therapy, >=2 weeks off PPI)
ignore the need for follow-up confirmation of eradication

edges_required_for_correct_answer: patient:duodenal_ulcer_h_pylori_positive --first_line_us_2024--> bismuth_quadruple_14d (or rifabutin triple, or vonoprazan-based options)

expected_sources_referenced: ACG Clinical Guideline: Treatment of Helicobacter pylori Infection — Chey et al., Am J Gastroenterol 2024
Maastricht VI / Florence Consensus on H. pylori — Malfertheiner et al., Gut 2022
FDA approval of vonoprazan (Voquezna) for H. pylori — 2022
